name: Heribert Illig
description: Heribert Illig is a German publisher and author best known for proposing the Phantom Time Hypothesis. This controversial theory posits that the Early Middle Ages, specifically the years 614 to 911 AD, were fabricated or misrepresented. Illig claims that these 297 years were added to the Gregorian calendar by the Holy Roman Emperor Otto III, Pope Sylvester II, and possibly Byzantine Emperor Constantine VII. The motivation, according to Illig, was to place these figures in the symbolic year 1000 AD. Illig argues that a lack of archaeological evidence and inconsistencies in dating methods support his hypothesis. He points to Romanesque architecture predating its supposed time period and the supposed lack of advancements during this period as further evidence. Mainstream historians strongly reject the Phantom Time Hypothesis, citing extensive documentary evidence, dendrochronology, radiocarbon dating, and other dating methods that contradict Illig's claims. They argue that Illig selectively interprets evidence and ignores vast amounts of contradictory information. The theory has been widely dismissed by the academic community, which considers it pseudoscience due to its lack of empirical support and reliance on flawed methodologies. Despite its rejection by experts, the Phantom Time Hypothesis has gained some popularity within alternative history circles.
subtitle: Proponent of the Phantom Time Hypothesis

claims.evidence: Lack of archaeological evidence, inconsistencies in dating methods, Romanesque architecture appearing too early.
claims.criticism: Extensive documentary evidence, dendrochronology, radiocarbon dating, and other dating methods contradict this claim. Illig selectively interprets evidence and ignores contradictory information.

claims.claim: Otto III, Pope Sylvester II, and Constantine VII conspired to add these years to the calendar.
claims.evidence: Desire to place themselves in the symbolic year 1000 AD.
claims.criticism: No evidence supports this conspiracy. The historical context and motivations are based on speculation and misinterpretations.

claims.claim: Romanesque architecture appeared earlier than conventionally dated.
claims.criticism: Mainstream dating methods, including dendrochronology and analysis of building materials, contradict Illig's dating of Romanesque structures. His analyses are often based on selective interpretations and disregard established architectural history.

keyFigures.name: Heribert Illig
keyFigures.role: Primary proponent of the Phantom Time Hypothesis

keyFigures.name: Rolf H. Bremmer Jr.
keyFigures.role: Critic who has written extensively debunking the Phantom Time Hypothesis

mythologyConnections: Symbolic importance of the year 1000 AD

timeline.event: Illig first publishes his Phantom Time Hypothesis.
Continued publication and promotion of the theory, alongside widespread criticism from historians and scientists.
